title: Datatilsynet (Denmark) - 2025-31-1972
text:
English Summary[edit | edit source]
Facts[edit | edit source]
A trade union (the representative) filed a complaint with the Danish DPA on behalf of one of its members (the data subject) about the data subject’s former employer (the controller) disclosing personal data to a third party in connection to a labour law dispute. The personal data included information on trade union membership.
The complaint claimed that the third party was related to the owner of the company where the data subject used to work and was a colleague of the data subject at the time the complaint was filed.
The representative of the data subject claimed that the controller did not have a legitimate interest for disclosing the personal data and that the disclosure of the trade union membership status was not necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim.
The controller alleged that it disclosed the information for the proper and effective handling of a claim made by the data subject with the third party acting as an advisor.

Holding[edit | edit source]
The DPA held the controller rightfully relied on its legitimate interest as a legal basis under Article 6(1)(f) GDPR for the disclosure of personal data of the data subject with the purpose of receiving advice from the third-party advisor in order to be able to take a position on the data subject’s labour law claim. 
Moreover, the DPA held that the controller lawfully disclosed the information on the trade union membership of the data subject under Article 9(2)(f) GDPR since the disclosure was necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim made by the data subject. 
Therefore, the DPA dismissed the complaint.

title: UODO (Poland) - DKN.5112.33.2022
text:
English Summary[edit | edit source]
Facts[edit | edit source]
The Polish DPA conducted an investigation into a company (the controller) in October 2022 in relation to the processing of its users’ (the data subjects) personal data via the controller’s application. 
The DPA found that, if fraud was suspected, the controller may ask a user to identify themselves by sending a photo of the front of the credit or debit card linked to their account or a photo of an ID card or passport.
The controller claimed it carried out the processing of personal data in accordance with Article 6(1)(f) GDPR based on its legitimate interest to verify the identity of a person suspected of fraud.

Holding[edit | edit source]
The DPA concluded that the controller violated several data protection provisions by processing the personal data of its users in an excessive and disproportionate manner when asking them to submit photos or scans of ID cards or passports for the purpose of identity verification. 
Specifically, the DPA found that the controller breached Article 6(1) GDPR by processing personal data without a valid legal basis. Therefore, the controller breached the principle of lawfulness under Article 5(1)(a) GDPR and the principle of data minimisation under Article 5(1)(c) GDPR. 
The DPA also held that the controller breached the principle of accountability in Article 5(2) GDPR. 
Therefore, the DPA fined the controller PLN 5,898,064 (approximately €1,392,768) and ordered the controller to bring its operations into compliance by ceasing the processing of those categories of data and deleting the data already collected.

title: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italy) - 10238270
text:
English Summary[edit | edit source]
Facts[edit | edit source]
In 2023 two NGOs and twelve individuals (the data subjects) filed a lawsuit against, among others, energy corporation Eni S.p.a. (the controller) over the alleged non-compliance with environmental legislation. The controller later published the statement of claims on its website, including the names, personal details, and social security numbers of the data subjects.
In response, the data subjects and one of the NGOs (Greenpeace) filed complaints with the DPA, claiming that their names were unlawfully published.
The controller later redacted the subject's data from the document available on its website and claimed that the publication was due to a human error.
In its defense, the controller also claimed that, given the media attention drawn by the lawsuit, it had a legitimate interest to both defend its public image and clarify the exact content of the lawsuit it was facing.
The controller also pointed out that some of the data it published was already publicly available and that the data subjects themselves had made it publicly known to the media that they were the claimants in its lawsuit. On these grounds, the controller argued that the data could be published.

Holding[edit | edit source]
The DPA first rejected the argument that the data were publicly available information. In this regard, the DPA noted that while some of the data subjects had publicly spoken about their lawsuit, they had never made their addresses and social security codes public. Furthermore, the DPA clarified that even publicly available data requires a legal basis for processing.
The DPA then held that the processing of the data was not justified by the controller’s legitimate interest for two reasons. First, the processing was not necessary, as the controller could have pursued its interest by publishing a redacted copy of the statement of claims. Second, the data subjects’ rights and freedoms outweighed the controller’s interest in the case at hand.
On these grounds, the DPA found a violation of Articles 5(1)(a) and 6(1) GDPR and fined the controller €96,000.

title: BVwG - W2522299666-1/6E
text:
English Summary[edit | edit source]
Facts[edit | edit source]
A public-law body (the data controller) sent a letter to an individual (the data subject) inviting them to receive a Covid-19 vaccination. On 14 December 2021, the data subject submitted a complaint with the Datenschutzbehörde (hereinafter DSB/data protection authority), alleging that the controller had violated their right to confidentiality of personal data under §1(2) DSG since they could not establish how the controller obtained information about their vaccination. 
Following a series of proceedings conducted against the wrong responsible party, the data subject identified the correct controller through a letter dated 01 August 2024. In particular, the data subject requested: (i) a finding of a violation of their fundamental rights; (ii) a prohibition of further data processing under §22(4) DSG; and (iii) the imposition of a fine.
Decision of the Data Protection Authority (DSB) 
By means of a decision rendered on 03 September 2024, the DSB upheld the complaint in part. Firstly, it held that the controller had unlawfully accessed and processed the data subject’s records from the central vaccination register and central patient index. In the absence of a legal basis in accordance with §1 para. 2 DSG, the DSB maintained that the data subject’s right to confidentiality was infringed upon. Secondly, the DSB dismissed the request for prohibition of processing given that the processing had already been completed and therefore fell short of the standard stipulated under §22 para 4 DSG. Thirdly, it rejected the request for a fine for two-fold reasons: the data subject held no subject right to demand one, and fines cannot be imposed on bodies of public law under §30(5) DSG. 
The data subject subsequently appealed the decision to the Federal Administrative Court on grounds that they found it “outrageous” that their vaccination data had been shared. Despite a court order issued on 02 October 2024 to clarify their grounds of appeal, the data subject did not submit further statements.

Holding[edit | edit source]
On standing to appeal 
The Federal Administrative Court held that a complainant lacks the necessary legal standing to appeal a DPA decision where the DPA has already fully ruled in their favour on the core issue. Since the DSB upheld the data subject’s complaint and found a violation of their right to confidentiality under §1(2) DSG, the data subject did not suffer legal grievances from that part of the decision and had no grounds to challenge it.  
On the prohibition of data processing  
The Court furthermore held that §22(4) DSG is a discretionary provision which requires a finding of imminent danger—namely, a significant and immediate threat to the data subject’s protected confidentiality interests—before a prohibition of processing may be ordered. This condition was not met because the processing had been brought to an end. Moreover, the Court maintained that §22(4) DSG does not confer a subjective legal entitlement on the data subject to demand such a measure. 
On the imposition of a fine 
As its last point, the Court held that fines cannot be imposed within the framework of administrative proceedings, and that no subjective right to demand the initiation of fine proceedings can be derived from Article 77(1) GDPR or §24(1) and (5) DSG. Additionally, the Court held that fines cannot be imposed on bodies of public law in accordance with §30 para. 5 DSG. In light of these reasons, the Court concluded that the data subject’s subjective rights were not violated. 
Overall conclusion 
Since the DSB’s decision had granted the data subject their rights in all the areas where they held a subjective right, and only dismissed or rejected their requests in realms where no such right existed, the prerequisites for a valid appeal were unfulfilled. The complaint was thus dismissed as inadmissible in its entirety. The Court further held that no appeal on points of law was admissible pursuant to Art. 133 para. 4 B-VG, as the decision rested on long-established and consistent case law of the Austrian Administrative Court and raised no legal question(s) of fundamental importance.

title: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italy) - 10233328
text:
Contents

1 English Summary

1.1 Facts
1.2 Holding

1.2.1 On the access request
1.2.2 On the storage of inactive email accounts
1.2.3 On the storage of email metadata







English Summary[edit | edit source]
Facts[edit | edit source]
The case involves an insurance company (the controller) and a former employee (the data subject).
After the end of the employment relationship, the data subject informally asked the controller to access his nominative email. At first, the controller denied access to the email account. Instead, the controller sorted out the contents of the account as “personal” and “non-personal communications” and provided the data subject with a copy of the “personal” communications only.
The data subject later reiterated his request by formally filing a request to receive a copy of all the contents of his email account. In the communications that followed, controller and data subject arranged to a meet in which the data subject would go through his email under supervision (allegedly to protect confidential information) and find the emails, of which he needed a copy.
Even after the meeting, the data subject was only provided with part of the data set: the controller witheld or redacted some of the data, allegedly to protect the data of third parties and to protect its trade secrets. So, the data subject filed a complaint with the DPA, claiming that his access request was not properly honored.
In its defense, the controller pointed to Article 15(4) GDPR: “the right to obtain a copy (...) shall not adversely affect the rights and freedoms of others”. In the controller’s view, the rights of others included both the protection of the data of third parties, and the protection of trade secrets. On these grounds, the controller claimed that it rightfully witheld certain data from the data subjecs.
In the procedure that followed, the DPA investigated not only the controller’s response to the access request but also its handling of employees’ email accounts in general as well as its policies for the retention of email metadata.

Holding[edit | edit source]
Overall, the DPA held that the controller violated Articles 5(1), 12, 13, 15, and 88 GDPR as well as Article 114 of the Italian data protection code. The DPA issued a €50,000 fine and ordered the controller to stop the infringements.

On the access request[edit | edit source]
First of all, the DPA clarified that the right to privacy, as sanctioned by Article 8 ECHR, also cover an invidual’s workplace email. For this reason, the controller had no grounds to pre-emptively sort out the contents of the data subject’s email account.
Likewise, the DPA clarified that the distinction between “personal” and “non-personal” emails was flawed. In the DPA’s view, even strictly professional emails constituted personal data for the data subject, as long as they had transited through his email. So, the data subject had a right to receive a copy of the data under the GDPR.
With regards to Article 15(4), the DPA conceded that a controller could, in principle, withold certain information from the response to an access request, in order to protect trade secrets or personal data data of third parties.
However, the DPA held that a controller may only rely on this exemption when it can prove, on a case-by-case basis, that honoring an access request would actually compromise trade secrets or the data of third parties[1]. The controller failed to do so in the case at hand: rather, it merely invoked the protection of trade secrets and the privacy of third parties as generic preoccupations. On these grounds, the DPA held that the controller unlawfully witheld data from the data subject.
The DPA also observed that redacting personal data from certain emails was entirely unnecessary, as the data subject was himself a part of the communications and, presumably, already knew the redacted names.
On these grounds, the DPA held that the controller unlawfully violated Article 12 and 15 GDPR.

On the storage of inactive email accounts[edit | edit source]
As mentioned above, the investigation also touched on the controller’s general handling of employees’ emails. During the procedure the DPA found that at the end of the employement relationship, the controller first deactivated the email accounts of former employees and then backed up the contents for five years.
The controller claimed that this storage period was both necessary and proportionate. In this regard, the controller noted that as an insurance company, it needed to preserve information in order to demonstrate compliance with sector-specific regulation.
The DPA, however, held that backups of email accounts are not suitable systems for document retention, as they cannot guarantee that documents remain unaltered. Furthermore, the DPA noted that such backups required the controller to sift through employees’ personal communication in order to recover documents. In the DPA’s eyes, this practice was excessively invasive of the privacy of former employees.
For these reasons, the DPA held that the controller violated Articles 5(1)(b), (c), and (e) GDPR. The DPA also found that the controller failed to inform employees that their inactive email accounts would be stored for five years., in violation of the controller’s transparency obligations under Article 13 GDPR.

On the storage of email metadata[edit | edit source]
The DPA finally looked into the controller’s handling of email metadata. The investigation found that the controller stored metadata logs for 12 months. The controller claimed that the practice was needed to both ensure the security of the company’s information system, and to defend the controller’s rights against “abuses”.
The DPA held that the controller held metadata for too long. Additionally, the DPA held that the controller violated Article 88 GDPR by processing metadata logs for purposes other than those, permitted under Italian labor law(!).
On these grounds, the DPA held that the controller violated Articles 5(1) and 88 GDPR as well as Article 114 of the Italian data protection code.


↑ In this regard, the DPA referred to EDPB guidance. See EDPB, 'Guidelines 01/2022 on data subject rights - Right of access', 28 March 2023 (version 2.1), margin 172 (available here).

title: BVwG - W252 2249249-1
text:
English Summary[edit | edit source]
Facts[edit | edit source]
The data subject had a heating supply contract with a private energy company, the controller. 
On 17 October 2019, the controller installed a smart heat meter in the data subject’s apartment. The device recorded various consumption and technical data, including energy usage, flow rates, and temperatures. It also stored historical operational data for error detection.
The controller read the meter once per year on-site. Although the device technically supported remote reading via a radio module, the controller did not use this function.
On 21 September 2020, the data subject filed a complaint with the DPA, arguing that the device unlawfully processed personal data and that the controller required a specific legal basis as a public authority. The DPA rejected the complaint on 11 October 2021. The data subject appealed to the court.

Holding[edit | edit source]
First, the court held that the controller was not a public authority. It acted as a private company under a contractual relationship and had no statutory powers to exercise authority. Therefore, Article 6(1)(f) GDPR could apply.
Second, the court held that the processing of consumption data (such as energy use, flow, and temperatures) complied with Article 6(1)(c) GDPR. National law required the collection and storage of such data for billing, energy efficiency, and system operation. This law satisfied the requirements of Article 6(3) GDPR by defining the purpose, scope, and retention periods.
Third, the court held that the processing of additional technical and historical data (such as error logs and operational records) complied with Article 6(1)(f) GDPR. The controller had a legitimate interest in ensuring the proper functioning and maintenance of the device. The processing was necessary for detecting faults and maintaining system reliability.
Finally, the court found that the data subject’s interests did not override the controller’s interests. Access to detailed historical data required specialised tools, and the data was only used in limited circumstances. The storage periods were also limited and aligned with legal requirements.
The court therefore upheld the DPA’s decision and rejected the appeal.

title: HDPA (Greece) - 5/2026
text:
English Summary[edit | edit source]
Facts[edit | edit source]
An employee (the data subject) submitted a request in June 2023 to a training and rehabilitation center for blind people (the controller), seeking access to his personal data in the form of CCTV footage, relating to a specific incident that occurred in the workplace. The request included adequate information that allowed the controller to identify the relevant recording. 
The controller did not satisfy the data subject’s request within one month, nor did they provide any notification to justify their inaction or inform about a potential delay in their response. Subsequently, the data subject lodged a complaint with the Hellenic Data Protection Authority (HDPA) on 9 January 2024. 
During the investigation, the DPA asked the controller to provide explanations about the complaint. Finally, the controller sent the recording from one camera after first blurring the faces depicted in it. However, the data subject complained further that the controller provided only partial access since the latter did not provide footage from a second camera, and the relevant material had been processed before being made available to him. In its defense, the controller referred to technical and organizational difficulties.

Holding[edit | edit source]
First, the DPA ruled that the controller did not satisfy the data subject’s request fully and within the prescribed time limits, thereby violating Article 15(1) and (3) GDPR in conjunction with Article 12(3) and (4) GDPR. For this infringement, the DPA imposed a fine of €5,000 on the controller. 
Second, the DPA issued a reprimand to the controller according to Article 58(2)(b) GDPR for violating Article 24 GDPR.
Third, the DPA ordered the controller to satisfy the access request of the data subject fully within 15 days, pursuant to Article 58(2)(d) GDPR.
